The main line is completely buckled from the ground shifting, and I can't find the shut-off valve.
I need to get these sump pumps running before the basement floods any further from the storm surge.

I've got a torch and some copper fittings, but I can't weld anything while the building is still swaying.
These burst pipes are spraying everywhere, and I can't find a dry spot to set my tool bag.
I don't care if the sky is turning purple as long as those main lines don't freeze.
If that glowing rift in the clouds causes a pressure drop, the whole basement is going to flood.
I've got a wrench in my hand and a leak in the stack, so I can't look up now.
This strange light is reflecting off the copper piping and making it impossible to see the coupling.
I hope whatever is happening up there doesn't shift the foundation and crack my new PVC installation.
Look, if you move that fence, you're going to crush the main sewer line running right under the flowerbed.
I can't just dig a trench for these new pipes if we aren't sure where your property line actually ends.
You've got a serious leak coming from the neighbor's side, so we need to settle this boundary before I start cutting.
If we put the cleanout valve on your side of the line, you're the one who has to maintain it.
I don't care who owns the dirt, I just need a clear path to reach that burst pipe without hitting a wall.
I don't care where that swirling hole leads, but it's causing a massive pressure drop in the main line.
If that glowing rift is leaking some kind of ethereal fluid, I'm going to need a whole new set of gaskets.
I've seen some nasty clogs in my time, but a dimensional tear in the basement is a first.
I can't tell if that portal is sucking air out of the pipes or if it's just a weird vacuum.
This isn't in the building code, and there is no way my insurance covers interdimensional plumbing failures.

I need to find a clear stream or a spring to check if the water pressure is sufficient.
These tangled vines are acting just like a massive clog in a main sewer line.
My boots are completely soaked through, but at least I have my waterproof sealant in the kit.
I hope there is a settlement nearby because I cannot fix a leak without a proper supply of fittings.
I can't hear a single drip or hiss of air in these pipes.
It's too quiet in this crawlspace to tell if that joint is leaking.
I've been waiting for the homeowner to answer me for ten whole minutes.
The silence makes me wonder if the main shutoff valve actually closed tight.
I'm just sitting here in the dark listening for a rattle that never comes.
You better back off before I use this heavy pipe wrench on your skull.
I don't care who you are, just get out of my way or things get messy.
If you touch my tools again, I'm going to shut off your air supply.
I've dealt with worse sewage than you, so your little threats don't scare me.
Step back right now or I'll make sure you never walk straight again.
This heavy-duty Ridgid pipe wrench has been in my kit for fifteen years and never slips.
I keep this professional grade drain snake polished and coiled because it saves me on every job.
My customized tool bag is the only one that holds every single fitting I need for a rush.
This high-end digital leak detector is the most expensive thing I own, but it pays for itself.
I cherish this old brass torch because it heats up a joint faster than any newer model.

If I use all the Teflon tape on this joint, I won't have any for the main valve.

I need to ration the sealant because the basement is flooding and I've only got one tube.
